identity.title: Other Support Activities for Water Transportation

lens.included_activities: Independent, repeat marine cargo checking, quantity and condition surveying, loading observation, hold-readiness inspection, cargo-damage documentation, and related compliance certificates for shipowners, charterers, cargo interests, insurers, and government shippers.
lens.excluded_activities: Floating drydock operation, ship dismantling, ship scaling and physical hull maintenance, shipyard repair, captive carrier or insurer inspection teams, classification-society statutory surveys outside this NAICS activity, shells, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy per-call, per-shipment, per-day, or master-service-agreement inspection and survey work, often around loading, discharge, loss prevention, claims, quantity verification, or regulatory evidence; repeat port and commodity relationships provide recurring demand even when individual assignments are episodic.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 488390 combines asset-heavy floating drydocks and physical ship scaling with professional marine cargo checking and surveying. Those models have fundamentally different occupation mixes, capital intensity, contracting, and substitution paths, so the lens retains the coherent independent cargo-inspection and survey segment rather than blending unlike businesses.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation and wage data for four-digit NAICS 488300, not six-digit 488390 or the narrowed marine-cargo-survey lens. I combine the parent industry's office, recordkeeping, planning, and inspection occupations with O*NET's transportation-inspector task list, then raise the exposed share relative to the parent because cargo survey work is document-, calculation-, image-, and report-intensive. The bridge may overstate exposure where assignments require continuous physical witnessing and manual sounding, and understate it where standardized cargo documents dominate time.
a.rationale: O*NET identifies transportation-inspector tasks directly relevant to marine surveyors: preparing reports, recording freight condition, reading loading and stability documents, calculating tonnage and capacity, checking conditions, and determining compliance. These are highly exposed to document extraction, calculation checks, structured report generation, image triage, and retrieval-augmented compliance support. Physical observation of loading, measurements, damage inspection, chain-of-custody evidence, and judgment in contested claims remain less substitutable. The base therefore treats almost half of wage-weighted current tasks as exposed, not the inspector role itself as replaceable.
a.change_evidence: Time-and-motion data from at least ten U.S. cargo survey firms, split by field attendance, travel, manual measurement, document review, calculation, evidence management, report drafting, client communication, and rework, matched to payroll by role.
caveats: The BLS industry proxy includes port operations, cargo handling, navigational services, drydock work, and other occupations outside this lens.
O*NET describes transportation inspectors across modes and does not report wage-weighted time by task.
Self-employed surveyors are excluded from OEWS, and small firms may have owners performing both field and administrative work.

rho.rationale: Report drafting, document comparison, calculation validation, photo indexing, scheduling, invoicing, and client updates can be integrated within five years using human review. Remote-inspection technology is already recognized by ABS, including drones, underwater vehicles, robotic crawlers, video, and digital documentation, which lowers technical adoption risk. Implementation remains limited by evidence integrity, offline shipboard conditions, cybersecurity, customer acceptance, inconsistent source documents, case-by-case survey eligibility, and the need for a qualified accountable professional to verify findings.
rho.change_evidence: Production telemetry from marine survey firms using AI-assisted reports and remote inspection tools, including adoption by workflow, field-to-report cycle time, correction rate, disputed-claim rate, insurer and customer acceptance, and avoided labor hours over at least 500 assignments.
caveats: Remote class surveys are adjacent to, but not identical with, independent cargo condition and quantity surveys.
Observed technology availability does not measure implementation share in U.S. LMM 488390 firms.
High-consequence or disputed cargo assignments may require conservative review and physical attendance.

e.rationale: The frozen denominator spans a residual industry that officially includes cargo checkers and surveyors, floating drydocks, ship scaling, and related non-shipyard services. Only the independent repeat cargo-inspection and survey firms fit the narrowed coherent lens. Eligibility is reduced for drydock and scaling companies, businesses dominated by one-off physical projects, captive inspection teams, nonprofit or delegated-authority structures, and owner-personal practices that lack a transferable operating organization.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of all 38 modeled LMM firms using business descriptions, customer and service mix, professional credentials, employee count, recurring-revenue share, ownership, and whether the operating platform can transfer independently of the lead surveyor.
caveats: No public source reports the share of 488390 LMM firms by cargo surveying, floating drydock, scaling, or other activity.
The 38-firm count is a frozen model estimate and may combine establishments belonging to larger firms.
Delegated survey and certification work may sit in nonprofit or recognized-organization structures unavailable for control acquisition.

s5.bridge: The SBA succession statistic concerns family-owned small businesses expecting retirement, not marine survey firms, while BizBuySell covers voluntary broker-reported transactions across 65 broad small-business industries and mostly much smaller companies. I use these sources to anchor retirement pressure and actual transfer activity, then lower the implied probability for qualifying transfers because surveyor reputation, credentials, customer trust, and owner dependence reduce saleability. The family-business proxy likely overstates five-year pressure for all eligible firms, while broker data misses private strategic transactions.
s5.rationale: SBA reports that 47% of family-business owners expecting to retire within five years lacked a successor. BizBuySell reported 2,117 businesses changed hands in Q2 2026, retirement was the leading planned-sale reason at 45%, and owner dependence and documented financials affected marketability. Independent marine survey practices plausibly face these succession forces, but credential and relationship concentration can lead to wind-downs rather than qualifying control transfers. A one-in-four five-year probability with a broad range is defensible absent industry deal data.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched set of PitchBook and GF Data transactions, Coast Guard or National Cargo Bureau office ownership changes, state corporate filings, and acquisitions by global testing-inspection-certification groups, combined with owner-age and succession interviews across at least 20 eligible firms.
caveats: Neither proxy isolates NAICS 488390, marine surveyors, or the $1-10M EBITDA band.
A retiring principal may close a personal practice or sell a client list without transferring an operating firm.
Reported marketplace deals skew smaller and exclude many private strategic acquisitions.

q.rationale: Per-assignment and fixed-fee survey pricing allows faster report production, reduced rework, and denser inspector scheduling to improve margins before contracts reset. Retention is limited by competitive bids, repeat-customer negotiation, transparent day rates, and sophisticated insurers, shippers, and commodity traders that can compare providers. Savings tied to faster turnaround, evidence quality, or lower claim leakage are more defensible than generic back-office savings. The base assumes meaningful but incomplete customer sharing over five discounted years.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice cohorts from at least ten cargo survey firms showing fee basis, travel and overtime treatment, master-agreement renewal cadence, bid win rates, client concentration, price changes, inspector utilization, and gross-margin evolution following workflow automation.
caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in marine cargo surveying.
P&I, government, commodity, and private shipper assignments have different pricing power and procurement intensity.
Faster work can create extra capacity that is valuable only if local assignment demand is available.

d5.bridge: BLS forecasts employment for all water-transportation workers across carrier and support industries, not constant-quality demand for marine cargo surveys. I use its 1% 2024-2034 employment growth and commodity-cycle commentary as a conservative demand anchor, then allow a slightly higher five-year base because cargo regulation, claims documentation, and inspection intensity need not move one-for-one with crews. Employment may understate service demand if productivity rises and may overstate it if survey work is automated or insourced.
d5.rationale: Cargo survey assignments depend on port calls, bulk and breakbulk volumes, claims, insurer requirements, and loading-risk complexity rather than total tonnage alone. BLS expects only 1% ten-year growth in water-transportation-worker employment and explicitly ties demand to bulk commodity cycles. At the same time, IMO rules preserve extensive planning, supervision, survey, and certification needs. The base is near flat with modest growth, while trade contraction and vessel-call consolidation support the downside and compliance intensity supports the upside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year forecasts and histories of U.S. bulk and breakbulk port calls, cargo tons by commodity, P&I cargo claims, National Cargo Bureau certificates, and survey assignments per call at the top 25 relevant ports.
caveats: Water-transportation employment is not a direct measure of survey assignments.
Containerized cargo may require less independent checking than bulk, project, food-aid, or disputed cargo.
Trade policy, commodity cycles, larger vessels, and fewer port calls can make national tonnage misleading.

o.bridge: USCG and IMO sources describe required or accepted surveys, certificates, qualified personnel, and recognized organizations for covered cargo and ship activities, while ABS sources describe adjacent class surveys and remote-inspection tools. I translate these into a high continuing need for an accountable survey organization, but reduce the share because customers and recognized bodies can insource data capture, use remote attendance, or automate routine evidence review. The regulatory sources may overstate operator persistence for voluntary commercial condition surveys and understate it for certificates accepted as compliance evidence.
o.rationale: USCG guidance states that third-party surveyors issue loading certificates used by P&I clubs and that National Cargo Bureau certificates can serve as compliance evidence; grain readiness also requires inspection of holds, structure, hazards, and stability calculations. IMO says surveys must be performed by administration officers, nominated surveyors, or recognized organizations, and cargo supervision requires qualified and experienced personnel. Remote technology can shift data collection and reduce travel, but ABS still conditions remote work on review, acceptance, recognized suppliers, and accountable surveyors. Most year-five quantity should therefore retain an operator, though with fewer field hours per assignment.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and procurement data from P&I clubs, shipowners, charterers, commodity traders, USDA shippers, and classification societies on five-year insourcing, remote-survey acceptance, certificate-signature rules, and assignments completed without an independent local survey firm.
caveats: The lens includes voluntary commercial surveys as well as compliance-adjacent certificates, so operator requirements vary.
Remote inspection can preserve the accountable firm while reducing local field labor, which belongs in exposure and implementation rather than demand elimination.
Classification-society surveys are adjacent and may compete with independent cargo surveyors rather than support them.

narrative.executive_view: Independent marine cargo survey and checking firms are a report-intensive, safety- and evidence-driven segment within a much broader residual NAICS code. AI can compress document review, calculations, photo handling, reporting, and coordination, while qualified human observation and accountability remain important. The opportunity is therefore an augmented-inspector model, not unattended certification. Firm availability is the most uncertain part because public data do not separate this segment from floating drydocks and ship scaling.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can ingest bills of lading, stowage plans, prior surveys, stability calculations, photos, and sensor readings; flag inconsistencies; draft structured reports; index evidence; translate communications; and automate scheduling and invoicing. Drones, crawlers, and remote video can reduce hazardous access and travel. Inspectors still need to witness loading, measure quantities and conditions, preserve evidentiary integrity, resolve exceptions, and sign accountable conclusions.
narrative.value_capture: Per-job and fixed-fee work can retain gains through faster turnaround and higher inspector utilization, especially when the firm wins on responsiveness and report quality. Retention weakens at competitive rebid, under transparent day rates, or when a concentrated insurer, government shipper, or commodity customer demands savings. A proprietary evidence workflow and consistent multi-port service matter more than access to a generic model.
narrative.firm_availability: The narrowed segment likely represents less than half of modeled LMM firms in this residual code, but there is no definitive roster. Survey firms may be transferable when they have multiple credentialed inspectors, repeat customers, standardized quality systems, and brand equity; single-principal practices may simply wind down. General small-business data show retirement-driven sale intent, but no marine-surveyor transfer series exists.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand should track bulk and breakbulk port calls, regulated cargoes, claims, and the need for independent evidence more than aggregate tonnage. Safety and certification frameworks continue to require qualified judgment, while remote capture changes delivery rather than eliminating most assignments. Downside comes from trade contraction, containerization, larger vessels with fewer calls, customer insourcing, or classification bodies absorbing adjacent work.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risks are code heterogeneity, owner and credential dependence, sparse six-digit occupation data, customer and port concentration, exposure to commodity cycles, liability for erroneous reports, cyber and evidence-integrity failures, and rapid remote-survey adoption by larger recognized organizations. A poor target would be a founder-led practice whose clients and signing authority do not survive the founder's exit.
narrative.principal_driver: Document- and evidence-heavy inspection workflows can be accelerated while qualified independent accountability remains valuable.
narrative.principal_weakness: The 488390 firm denominator mixes incompatible business models, and the eligible survey-firm share is not observed.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 488390 Other Support Activities for Water Transportation
sources.supports: Official residual-industry scope and illustrative examples: floating drydocks, ship scaling, and marine cargo checkers and surveyors.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: NAICS 488300 Support Activities for Water Transportation
sources.supports: Parent-industry occupation employment and wage mix, including office support, dispatch, inspectors, managers, water-transportation workers, maintenance, and material-moving roles.

sources.title: O*NET: Transportation Inspectors
sources.supports: Marine cargo surveyor task proxy: physical inspection, observing loading, reports, records, calculations, document review, compliance judgment, measurements, and damage assessment.

sources.title: U.S. Coast Guard Foreign Freight Vessel Examiner Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures
sources.supports: Loading certificates follow independent survey; P&I use; National Cargo Bureau certificates as compliance evidence; grain readiness, hold, stability, and cargo inspection tasks.

sources.title: IMO Code of Safe Practice for Cargo Stowage and Securing
sources.supports: Cargo safety requires proper planning, execution and supervision by qualified and experienced personnel, with the master retaining responsibility.

sources.title: IMO Surveys, Verifications and Certification
sources.supports: Ships require periodic survey; inspections may be entrusted to nominated surveyors or recognized organizations under the applicable legal framework.

sources.title: ABS Guidance for Remote Inspection Technologies
sources.supports: Operational availability of drones, underwater vehicles, robotic crawlers, high-definition imaging, data-management standards, training, and limitations for marine inspections.

sources.title: ABS Remote Survey
sources.supports: Remote surveys use digital evidence and recognized service suppliers but remain subject to ABS review, case-by-case eligibility, flag approval, and accountable surveyor acceptance.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Water Transportation Workers
sources.supports: Water-transportation work, credential and physical requirements, 1% employment growth forecast for 2024-2034, and sensitivity to bulk commodity demand.

sources.title: SBA: 5 Challenges for Family-Owned Businesses
sources.supports: Succession proxy: 47% of family-business owners expecting retirement within five years reportedly lacked a successor.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report: Q2 2026
sources.supports: Transfer-market proxy: 2,117 Q2 2026 transactions, retirement as the leading planned-sale reason at 45%, owner preparedness and financing constraints, and voluntary broker reporting across broad industries.
